tools.name: Tsunami IRC Botnet

ttps.evidence_snippet: : File Deletion, T1190 - Exploit Public-Facing Application
T1552.001 - Unsecured Credentials: Credentials in Files
T1027 - Obfuscated Files or Information: Steganography
T1562.008 - Impair Defenses: Disable Cloud Logs

summary: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 Cloud Threat Report Volume 7 (published ~March 2023; data Jan 2022-Jan 2023). Analysed 210,000 cloud accounts across 1,300+ organisations on all major CSPs. Key findings: 83% orgs have hard-coded SCM creds; 85% in VM user data; 76% don't enforce MFA for console users; 145hr avg alert resolution (60% >4 days); 66% storage buckets contain sensitive data (63% publicly exposed). Two anonymised IR scenarios: (1) SIM-swap → IAM key theft from cloned SCM repos → extortion without encryption (data leaked on dark web); (2) CVE-2021-40438 SSRF + IMDSv1 → cloud creds → CloudFormation cryptojacking botnet ($12,000/day GPU costs). Cloud threat actors updated: PurpleUrchin (130,000 accounts, CAPTCHA bypass, GitHub/Heroku/Togglebox abuse), Kinsing (PostgreSQL/K8s; syslog deletion; T1525 added), WatchDog (steganography-masked malware; T1584.004/T1027 added), 8220 (30,000-host botnet; Tsunami IRC). OSS risks: Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) still rising; Spring4Shell (CVE-2022-22965); 7,300+ malicious OSS packages; 51% codebases >100 dependencies; 77% vulns from non-root packages. CNCF: DoS/auth-bypass = 57% of vulns.

timeline.event: Data collection period begins; Log4Shell exploitation trends tracked from January 2022
